LRT: Lineage estimation by integrative analysis of single cell RNA-seq and TCR-seq data

Juan Xie

2022-04-04

Contents

  • 1 Introduction
  • 2 Dataset
  • 3 Load packages
  • 4 Data preprocessing
  • 5 Combine scTCR-seq and scRNA-seq data
  • 6 Display the distribution of a particular clonotype on UMAP
  • 7 Infer clonotype lineages
  • 8 Lineage clustering
  • 9 Session Info

1 Introduction

This vignette will demonstrate a full clonotype lineage analysis workflow, including preprocessing,lineage inference, clustering and visualization.

2 Dataset

We will demonstrate the usage of LRT via a real dataset from Khatun et al.This dataset consists of scTCR-seq data and scRNA-seq data from five mice. The raw data can be accessed at https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/geo/query/acc.cgi?acc=GSE158896.

3 Load packages

The LRT package has multiple dependencies, to use it smoothly, it is advised to load these dependencies.

library(LRT)
library(Seurat)
library(slingshot)
library(tidyverse)
library(TrajectoryUtils)
library(progress)
library(shiny)
library(shinyBS)

4 Data preprocessing

To begin our analysis of the clonotye lineage, we need to prepare a dataframe containing clonotype information and a seurat object containing cell id, UMAP coordinates and clustering labels.

An example R code is provided at https://github.com/JuanXie19/LRT/blob/master/preprocess.r, which illustrates the data pre-processing step and prepares the input for our analysis.

5 Combine scTCR-seq and scRNA-seq data

After pre-processing, we need to attach the clonotype information from scTCR-seq to the seurat object.

For the clonotype information,you can choose anything you like, as long as it can be used to define clonotype. Common choices are CDR3 amino acid/nucleotide sequence (either \(\alpha\) chain, \(\beta\) chain or paired \(\alpha \beta\) chain). Note that the clonotype information must be saved in a column named ‘cdr3’ in the scTCR-seq data. An example pre-processed seurat object is provided at https://drive.google.com/file/d/1xrPXm51pX62sE6AaXalT-iVjpwq5ASIi/view?usp=sharing, and example scTCR-seq data is provided at https://drive.google.com/file/d/1P80GIvhpcZS6ss3m-gf4MwYFJw8UQiKg/view?usp=sharing. Users can download these example data and explore the functions.

To combine scTCR-seq and scRNA-seq data, users can simply call the getCombinedDataSet function.

# load TCR data
TCR <-read.csv('/path/to/scTCR-seqData/TCR.csv') 

# load seurat object with clustering labels and UMAP coordinates
load('Mice.sub.rda') 

# combine TCR and scRNA-seq
Combined <- getCombinedDataSet(TCR, Mice.sub)

The combined dataset is an updated Seurat object. Users could directly access the object via @seurat. They can also easily access the components (UMAP coordinates, seurat cluster labels and TCR sequences) by calling the corresponding accessor functions:

# have a look at the combined dataset
Combined
#> An object of class "CombinedDataSet"
#> Slot "seurat":
#> An object of class Seurat 
#> 45449 features across 7395 samples within 3 assays 
#> Active assay: integrated (3000 features, 3000 variable features)
#>  2 other assays present: RNA, SCT
#>  3 dimensional reductions calculated: pca, umap, tsne

# Visualization--DimPlot
DimPlot(Combined@seurat)


# access the cdr3 for first 3 cells
getTCRs(Combined)[1:3]
#> M1_AAACCTGCAGTGACAG-1 M1_AAACCTGTCAAAGTAG-1 M1_AAACGGGCACCGAATT-1 
#>         "CAWSGRDEQYF"      "CASSDRGPNERLFF"        "CAWSLVGDTQYF"

# access the clustering labels for first 3 cells
seuratClusterLabels(Combined)[1:3]
#> M1_AAACCTGCAGTGACAG-1 M1_AAACCTGTCAAAGTAG-1 M1_AAACGGGCACCGAATT-1 
#>             Th1_inter                   Th1                  Tcmp 
#> Levels: Tcmp Th1_inter Th1 Pre_Th

# the UMAP coordinates for the first 3 cells
LRT::reducedDim(Combined)[1:3,]
#>                           UMAP_1    UMAP_2
#> M1_AAACCTGCAGTGACAG-1 -4.0101512 -1.682457
#> M1_AAACCTGTCAAAGTAG-1 -6.0744331 -2.266358
#> M1_AAACGGGCACCGAATT-1  0.2896429  3.739995

6 Display the distribution of a particular clonotype on UMAP

After combining the data, users can dynamically and interactively explore the distribution of a particular clonotype on the UMAP space by calling shinyClone function.

The shinyClone will trigger a Shiny app, which shows a table of clonotype, along with the relevant information, e.g., how many cells are associated with each clonotype and how these cells are distributed among cell clusters. If the user clicks a clonotype in this table, they can also check the distribution of cells associated with each clonotype on the UMAP plot. The plot can also be downloaded by clicking the button below the plot.

# explore the distribution of clonotypes
shinyClone(Combined)

shinyClone

7 Infer clonotype lineages

Users can infer clonotype lineages using the getClonotypeLineages function. Users can control the ways that the lineages are inferred (for details, please refer to the function documentation). One thing to note is that it is advised to specify the root of the lineage, as the order of lineage is very important for the later lineage clustering step.

The inferred lineages can be accessed via calling the lrtLineages function. Users can also take a look at the parameters via calling lrtParams function.

# infer lineages. Here use Tcmp as the root
Trajectory <- getClonotypeLineages(Combined,start.clus = 'Tcmp', end.clus = NULL, dist.method = 'simple', use.median = TRUE)

# Access one of the inferred lineages
lrtLineages(Trajectory)[[1]]
#>            UMAP_1     UMAP_2
#> Tcmp   -0.3576873  0.9466877
#> Pre_Th -0.4850266 -3.3026153
#> Th1    -5.6796764 -2.4186774

# Access the parameters
lrtParams(Trajectory)
#> $start.clus
#> [1] "Tcmp"
#> 
#> $end.clus
#> NULL
#> 
#> $dist.method
#> [1] "simple"
#> 
#> $use.median
#> [1] TRUE

8 Lineage clustering

It is noted that some lineages are grouped together, indicating that some clonotypes may share similar trajectory. To find such groups, users could apply shinyClust function to conduct clustering analysis.

# prepare the TCR info for shinyClust
G.df <- getShinyInput(Combined)

# clust the lineages
shinyClust(Trajectory,G.df)

The shinyClust will trigger another Shiny app that allows users to intuitively and dynamically conduct clustering analysis of clonotype lineages. Whenever usres specify the desired number of clusters, shinyClust automatically update clustering results. If the user chooses a particular cluster to display, shinyClust updates (i) a lineage plot, which shows clonotype trajectories corresponding to the lineage cluster on the UMAP plot (plot on the left, titled ‘Lineages’), and (ii) a cell distribution plot, which shows the cells associated with this lineage cluster on the UMAP plot (plot on the right, titled ‘Cell distribution’) . shinyClust also provides various options to fine tune the clustering results, e.g., clustering algorithm (hierarchical or partitioning clustering) and clustering parameters, among others. All the plots can also be downloaded by clicking the buttons below the plots.

The main interface looks like the following:

9 Session Info
